summary: Bryan Cantrill celebrates John Birrell's progress porting DTrace to FreeBSD, noting that while the port isn't complete, it's already useful for production systems and essential for kernel development. He frames the port as a triple win: FreeBSD users gain powerful observability tools, DTrace users get another platform and larger community, and OpenSolaris is validated as a genuinely open platform contributing to the open source ecosystem.
key_points: John Birrell has core DTrace functionality working on FreeBSD after months of work
The port is already useful for production FreeBSD systems and essential for kernel developers
DTrace on FreeBSD validates OpenSolaris as a truly open platform
Cross-platform ports benefit all communities involved through shared experiences
money_quotes: Once you've done kernel development with DTrace, there's no going back.
If you run FreeBSD in production, you're going to want John's port as it stands today – and if you develop for the FreeBSD kernel (drivers or otherwise), you're going to need it.

The presence of a FreeBSD port of DTrace validates that OpenSolaris is an open, innovative platform (despite what some buttheads say) – one that will benefit from and contribute to the undeniable economics of open source.
key_insight: Cross-platform open source ports create virtuous cycles that benefit all participating communities while validating the openness of the originating project.
